title: Cambridge Central School District
text:
Overview
This website looks at the Cambridge Central School District (Cambridge CSD, Washington County, New York) alongside nearby districts, using public data from the New York State Education Department (NYSED) and selected other sources. It is one of several analyses in the ccs project; the aim is to put Cambridge’s numbers in context rather than to view them in isolation.

title: Cambridge Central School District
text:
Comparison groups
Different questions call for different peer sets, so this website uses two groups that can be adjusted as the work develops:

Graph group (5 districts) — used for charts, where a short list keeps lines legible: Cambridge, Greenwich, Salem (all Washington County), Schuylerville (Saratoga County), and Hoosick Falls (Rensselaer County). These are nearby small districts.
Table group (12 districts) — used for tables, which can hold more rows: every Washington County K-12 district plus the two graph-group districts that sit just outside the county (Schuylerville and Hoosick Falls).

The table group is the broader set; the graph group is a readable subset of it. Putnam CSD is left out of the “Washington County K-12” set because it is a K-8 district – it has no high school of its own and pays to send its high-school students elsewhere, so it is not comparable on K-12 measures.







Group
Districts




Graph (charts)
Cambridge, Greenwich, Hoosick Falls, Salem, Schuylerville


Table (tables)
The graph group + Argyle, Fort Ann, Fort Edward, Granville, Hartford, Hudson Falls, Whitehall

title: Cambridge Central School District
text:
Data and a few cautions
The underlying numbers come from NYSED’s public downloads and other reputable sources; each chapter and the Sources appendix give the specifics. A few things to keep in mind throughout:

Year labels are school-year-ending years. “2025” means the 2024–25 school year. Enrollment is the BEDS-day count (first Wednesday of October).
Teacher counts begin in 2017–18. So time series that depend on teachers (such as students per teacher) start at school-year-ending 2018, even though enrollment runs back to 2015–16.
Students per teacher is an approximation, not official class size. It divides K-12 enrollment by a district-wide teacher headcount (all grades, not full-time-equivalents). It is useful for comparison across districts and over time, but it is not NYSED’s official pupil-teacher or average-class-size figure. See the Sources appendix for detail.
Charter schools are excluded — they have no district-total row in the source data — so these are district-operated figures.

title: 1  Demographics: Rural aging and population decline
text:
Cambridge is caught in a storm buffeting rural school districts throughout New York.
Population is declining in rural areas throughout New York.
The main reasons for this decline are:

Declines in the number of births, which is a statewide trend
Out-migration of younger adults, leading to an aging population
Economic factors that make rural areas less attractive for new residents

The charts below put Cambridge’s county — Washington County — alongside its neighbors on both sides of the state line. Each county’s population is shown relative to its own starting point (indexed to 100), so the lines compare trends, not sizes. Washington County is the red line in each chart.


Code
g = _indexed(pop.filter(pl.col("year").is_between(2000, 2020)), base_year=2000)
order = [COUNTY_LABEL[c] for c in ["Washington", "Saratoga", "Warren", "Rensselaer",
                                   "Bennington County", "Rutland County"]]

(
    ggplot(g, aes("year", "index", color="county"))
    + geom_line(size=1)
    + scale_color_manual(values={COUNTY_LABEL[k]: v for k, v in COUNTY_PALETTE.items()},
                         limits=order)
    + scale_x_continuous(breaks=list(range(2000, 2021, 5)))
    + scale_y_continuous(limits=(80, 120), breaks=list(range(80, 121, 5)))
    + labs(
        x=None, y="Population (2000 = 100)",
        title="Population by county, indexed to 2000",
        subtitle="2000–2020 (100 = each county's 2000 population)",
    )
    + cc.theme_ccs()
)








Figure 1.1: Total population indexed to 2000 (=100), 2000–2020. Values below 100 mean a county ended the period smaller than it began. NY counties from the popfc forecast project (reconciled Census/NYSDOL series); Bennington and Rutland from the U.S. Census Bureau (Population Estimates Program). The 2020 uptick for the two Vermont counties is the 2020 Census rebenchmark — the census counted more residents than the prior estimates had shown.







Code
g = _indexed(pop.filter(pl.col("year").is_between(2020, 2035)), base_year=2020)
order = [COUNTY_LABEL[c] for c in ["Washington", "Saratoga", "Warren", "Rensselaer"]]

hist = g.filter(pl.col("period") == "history")          # 2020–2024
# Forecast segment, anchored at the 2024 point so the dashed line meets the solid one:
fc = pl.concat([g.filter(pl.col("year") == 2024), g.filter(pl.col("period") == "forecast")])

(
    ggplot()
    + geom_line(hist, aes("year", "index", color="county"), size=1)
    + geom_line(fc, aes("year", "index", color="county"), linetype="dashed", size=1)
    + geom_vline(xintercept=2024.5, color="#999999", size=0.6)
    + annotate("text", x=2025, y=118.5, label="forecast →",
               ha="left", color="#666666", size=9)
    + scale_color_manual(values={COUNTY_LABEL[k]: v for k, v in COUNTY_PALETTE.items()},
                         limits=order)
    + scale_x_continuous(breaks=list(range(2020, 2036, 5)))
    + scale_y_continuous(limits=(85, 120), breaks=list(range(85, 121, 5)))
    + labs(
        x=None, y="Population (2020 = 100)",
        title="Projected population by county, indexed to 2020",
        subtitle="2020–2035 (solid = history, dashed = forecast)",
    )
    + cc.theme_ccs()
)








Figure 1.2: Total population indexed to 2020 (=100), 2020–2035. Solid lines are history (through 2024); dashed lines are the popfc baseline forecast (2025–2035). NY counties only — Vermont has no forecast in popfc.





The table below breaks Washington County’s annual components of change into its parts — births, deaths, and migration — so the decline can be traced to its drivers. Negative migration means more people moved out than in.


Code
tbl = (
    components
    .filter(pl.col("year") >= 2021)           # 2020 is a partial Census period (see note)
    .with_columns(
        pl.col("births").round(0).cast(pl.Int64),
        pl.col("deaths").round(0).cast(pl.Int64),
        pl.col("domestic_mig").round(0).cast(pl.Int64),
        pl.col("international_mig").round(0).cast(pl.Int64),
        pl.col("net_mig").round(0).cast(pl.Int64),
        pl.col("pop_change").round(0).cast(pl.Int64),
        pl.when(pl.col("period") == "forecast").then(pl.lit(" (fc)")).otherwise(pl.lit(""))
        .alias("tag"),
    )
    .with_columns((pl.col("year").cast(pl.Utf8) + pl.col("tag")).alias("year"))
    .drop("tag", "period")
)

mig_cols = ["domestic_mig", "international_mig", "net_mig", "pop_change"]

(
    GT(tbl, rowname_col="year")
    .tab_header(
        title="Washington County: components of population change",
        subtitle="Annual, 2021–2035 (signed; negative = loss)",
    )
    .tab_spanner(label="Migration", columns=["domestic_mig", "international_mig", "net_mig"])
    .cols_label(
        births="Births", deaths="Deaths",
        domestic_mig="Domestic", international_mig="International",
        net_mig="Net", pop_change="Total change",
    )
    .fmt_integer(columns=["births", "deaths"])
    .fmt_integer(columns=mig_cols, rows=pl.col("year").is_not_null())
    # Forecast years have no domestic/international split → show "—" not "None".
    .sub_missing(columns=["domestic_mig", "international_mig"], missing_text="—")
    .cols_align(columns=mig_cols, align="right")
    .tab_source_note(
        md(
            "**Source:** Washington County components from the **popfc** forecast "
            "project (history: U.S. Census Bureau Population Estimates Program, "
            "v2025 vintage, 2021–2025; forecast: popfc baseline cohort-component "
            "model, 2026–2035). **2020 is omitted** because the 2020 Census "
            "boundary makes that year a partial April–July period in the source. "
            "Domestic/international migration are not projected separately beyond "
            "2025, so only net migration is shown for forecast years."

crumbs: CCS and the challenges it faces
1  Demographics: Rural aging and population decline

title: 2  Enrollment is declining
text:
2.1 What this means
Cambridge has fewer students than it used to — about 124 fewer K-12 students in 2024–25 than in 2015–16, a decline of roughly 14%.
Fewer students mean:
The figure below shows how Cambridge’s K-12 enrollment has changed since 2015–16, alongside nearby districts for context.
The districts differ greatly in size, which is why the chart above clips Schuylerville (1,645 students in 2016) off the top while bunching Salem (512) near the bottom. Setting each district’s 2016 enrollment equal to 100 puts them on one shared scale, so the lines compare rates of change rather than size:
Cambridge’s enrollment decline is real, but it is not alone. The trend is regional. Schuylerville and Greenwich have seen larger absolute losses (327 and 219 students respectively), while Hoosick Falls and Salem have held steadier. The pandemic year 2020–21 shows a sharp dip across districts, followed by partial recoveries that did not fully return to pre-pandemic levels.
The table below shows where each district stood in 2015–16 and 2024–25, and how much they gained or lost over the decade.
Cambridge lost 124 students (−14%) over the decade, the second-largest decline among the five districts after Schuylerville’s 327-student loss (−20%). Greenwich lost 219 students (−21%). Salem and Hoosick Falls held closer to flat.
Enrollment decline sets the context for everything else in Cambridge’s budget:
The enrollment trend is a regional challenge, not unique to Cambridge. But the scale of the decline — and the decisions it forces about what to preserve — is what Cambridge’s community must grapple with.
See the exploratory Class size — all measures chapter for deeper analysis of staffing ratios and class-size measures.
crumbs: CCS and the challenges it faces

title: 2  Enrollment is declining
text:
State aid is tied to students. Fewer students mean less foundation aid from New York State, unless the state adjusts its formulas.
Fixed costs don’t shrink. A building, a bus route, a principal — these costs don’t automatically fall when enrollment does. Spreading fixed costs over fewer students is part of why Cambridge’s spending per pupil ranks high (see the Spending chapter).
Staffing choices get harder. Cambridge now has the fewest students per teacher of the comparison group (see the Class size chapter) — a choice that preserves small classes but adds pressure at budget time.
crumbs: CCS and the challenges it faces

title: 3  Class size and staffing
text:
3.1 What this measure is (and isn’t)
Cambridge now has fewer students per teacher than any nearby district in the comparison group. In 2024–25, Cambridge had about 8.7 students per teacher — the lowest ratio of the five, and below the state average. This measure — K-12 students per teacher — is a district-wide staffing ratio: the total K-12 enrollment divided by the number of teachers. It is not NYSED’s official class size (which counts only specific classrooms), but it is a fair way to compare districts and track change over time.
The chart below shows how students per teacher has changed since teacher counts became available (2017–18 onward).
Cambridge’s ratio has fallen over time, driven by two trends from the Enrollment chapter: enrollment dropped while teacher counts held roughly steady. In 2017–18, Cambridge had 869 students and 85 teachers (10.2 students per teacher). By 2024–25, enrollment had fallen to 741 while teachers remained at 85 — pushing the ratio down to 8.7.
Only Salem had a lower ratio in 2017–18. Cambridge crossed below Salem around 2022–23 and now sits at the bottom of the group.
Students per teacher is a staffing ratio, not a class-size count. It divides K-12 enrollment by the number of teachers in the district, which includes:
This makes it an approximate measure for comparing districts and tracking change — useful for the big picture, but not the same as the number of children in a typical classroom. NYSED’s official class-size figures cover only a handful of specific grades and subjects, and they exist only since 2018–19. For a deeper dive into those measures, see the exploratory Class size — all measures chapter.
crumbs: CCS and the challenges it faces

title: 3  Class size and staffing
text:
All teachers, not just those leading regular classes (so art, music, special education, and other specialists are counted)
Teacher headcount (the number of people employed), not full-time-equivalents (so part-time teachers count as one each)
All grades K-12, not just the grades NYSED reports for class size
crumbs: CCS and the challenges it faces

title: 3  Class size and staffing
text:
3.2 What it means for Cambridge
Cambridge’s low students-per-teacher ratio reflects a choice: maintain small classes and preserve programs even as enrollment falls. That choice has implications:

Higher spending per pupil. Fewer students to spread fixed costs (salaries, benefits, facilities) across means spending per student runs higher (see the Spending chapter).
Budget pressure. The same tax base must support more teachers per student than neighboring districts, which can strain the budget when state aid doesn’t keep pace.
Classroom experience. Fewer students per teacher means more individual attention, which is a strength Cambridge has maintained even as enrollment declined.

The table below shows where each district stood in 2017–18 and 2024–25, and how much their staffing ratios changed.


Code
g = cc.panel_for(cc.GRAPH_GROUP, since=2018).filter(
    pl.col("year_end").is_in([2018, 2025])
)

wide = (
    g.with_columns(
        pl.when(pl.col("region") == "Washington County").then(0).otherwise(1).alias("_rank")
    )
    .pivot(values="k12_students_per_teacher", index=["_rank", "region", "district"], on="year_end")
    .sort(["_rank", "district"])
    .drop("_rank")
)

wide = wide.with_columns(
    (pl.col("2025") - pl.col("2018")).alias("change")
)

(
    GT(wide, rowname_col="district", groupname_col="region")
    .tab_header(
        title="Students per teacher change",
        subtitle="School-year-ending 2018 (2017–18) and 2025 (2024–25), with change"
    )
    .fmt_number(columns=["2018", "2025", "change"], decimals=1)
    .tab_spanner(label="School year ending", columns=["2018", "2025"])
    .cols_label(change=md("Change<br>2018→2025"))
    .tab_style(
        style=[style.fill(color="#FCEFB4"), style.text(weight="bold")],
        locations=[
            loc.body(rows=pl.col("district") == cc.FOCUS),
            loc.stub(rows=pl.col("district") == cc.FOCUS),
        ],
    )
    .tab_source_note(
        md(
            "**Source:** NYSED Enrollment database (K-12 enrollment) and "
            "Student & Educator database (teacher counts). Ratio = district "
            "K-12 enrollment ÷ teacher headcount; an approximate comparison "
            "measure, not official class size. Year = school-year-ending "
            "(2025 = 2024–25)."
        )
    )
)

crumbs: CCS and the challenges it faces

title: 4  Spending per pupil
text:
4.1 Why the smallest districts spend the most per pupil
Cambridge spends more per student than most nearby districts — consistently second only to Salem. In 2024–25, Cambridge spent roughly $31,000 per K-12 pupil in current operating funds, compared to a group average of about $27,000. This measure — current operating spending per pupil — captures what the district actually spends to educate each student: salaries, benefits, classroom services, BOCES programs, and federally funded services.
The chart below shows how spending per pupil has changed since 2015–16.
Every district’s spending per pupil has risen over the decade. Part of this is inflation — the dollars are not adjusted for rising prices. But the rank order is consistent: Cambridge and Salem, the two smallest districts by enrollment, sit at the top year after year.
The pattern above is the cost-side mirror of the enrollment story. Small districts spread fixed costs over fewer students. A principal, a building, a bus route — these costs don’t shrink linearly when enrollment drops. So a district with 500 students inevitably spends more per pupil than a district with 1,300 students, even if they offer similar programs.
Cambridge and Salem are the smallest in the comparison group, so their higher spending per pupil is not a sign of inefficiency — it’s the arithmetic of small scale. The question for Cambridge is whether its level of spending is sustainable given its enrollment decline and tax base (discussed in the Taxes chapter).

title: 4  Spending per pupil
text:
4.2 What this measure includes (and excludes)
The chart uses current operating spending, which deliberately leaves out two categories that would make year-to-year comparisons misleading:

Capital projects and debt. Building a new school or paying off long-term debt arrives in large, irregular lumps. Including these would make spending per pupil volatile in ways that don’t reflect the actual cost of educating students.
Transfers between a district’s own funds. Moving money from one fund to another doesn’t change total resources, so it’s excluded to avoid double-counting.

This is close to the “current spending per pupil” measure the U.S. Census and NCES use to compare districts and states. The dollars come from the New York State Comptroller’s financial-reporting data and are not adjusted for inflation, so part of the upward drift reflects rising prices rather than rising real resources.
The table below shows where each district stood in 2015–16 and 2024–25, and how much their spending per pupil changed.


Code
t = cc.spending_for(cc.GRAPH_GROUP).filter(pl.col("year_end").is_between(2016, 2025))

wide = (
    t.with_columns(
        pl.when(pl.col("region") == "Washington County").then(0).otherwise(1).alias("_rank")
    )
    .pivot(values="current_pp", index=["_rank", "region", "district"], on="year_end")
    .sort(["_rank", "district"])
    .drop("_rank")
)

wide = wide.with_columns((pl.col("2025") - pl.col("2016")).alias("chg"))

(
    GT(wide, rowname_col="district", groupname_col="region")
    .tab_header(
        title="Current operating spending per pupil",
        subtitle="School-year-ending 2016 (2015–16) and 2025 (2024–25), nominal dollars"
    )
    .fmt_currency(columns=["2016", "2025", "chg"], decimals=0)
    .tab_spanner(label="School year ending", columns=["2016", "2025"])
    .cols_label(chg=md("Change<br>2016→2025"))
    .tab_style(
        style=[style.fill(color="#FCEFB4"), style.text(weight="bold")],
        locations=[
            loc.body(rows=pl.col("district") == cc.FOCUS),
            loc.stub(rows=pl.col("district") == cc.FOCUS),
        ],
    )
    .tab_source_note(
        md(
            "**Source:** New York State Comptroller local-government financial "
            "data (expenditures); K-12 enrollment from NYSED. Current operating "
            "spending = General, Special Aid and School Lunch funds, excluding "
            "capital outlay, debt service and interfund transfers. Nominal "
            "dollars; year = school-year-ending (2025 = 2024–25)."
        )
    )
)

For additional detail on spending measures, see the exploratory Spending chapter.

title: 5  Property taxes
text:
5.1 The tax rate: what property owners pay per value
Cambridge’s property tax levy — the total amount of property tax the district collects — has risen from about $8.2 million in 2014–15 to $9.6 million in 2023–24. That’s a 17% increase over a decade, driven by both higher spending (see the Spending chapter) and changes in the local tax base.
The chart below shows how Cambridge’s tax levy has changed since 2014–15, alongside nearby districts for context.
Cambridge’s levy has risen steadily, but not as sharply as some neighboring districts. Schuylerville and Hoosick Falls, both larger, have seen larger absolute increases — which makes sense given they serve more students. Salem’s levy has been nearly flat, reflecting its stable enrollment.
The levy is what the district collects. The full-value tax rate is what property owners pay per $1,000 of taxable full value — the standard way to compare tax effort across places with different assessment practices.
A higher tax rate means property owners pay more for each dollar of property value. The chart below shows how Cambridge’s full-value tax rate has changed since 2014–15.
Code
def tax_rate_for(group: dict[str, str]) -> pl.DataFrame:
    """Full-value tax rate for group, labelled like other panels."""
    region_by_cd = {
        cd: ("Washington County" if cd in cc.WASHINGTON_K12 else "Other comparison districts")
        for cd in group
    }
    return (
        osc_totals.filter(pl.col("nysed_district_cd").is_in(list(group)))
        .with_columns(
            pl.col("nysed_district_cd").replace_strict(group).alias("district"),
            pl.col("nysed_district_cd").replace_strict(region_by_cd).alias("region"),
        )
        .select("district", "region", "year_end", "full_value_tax_rate")
        .sort("district", "year_end")
    )

g = tax_rate_for(cc.GRAPH_GROUP)
cambridge = g.filter(pl.col("district") == cc.FOCUS)

(
    ggplot(g, aes("year_end", "full_value_tax_rate", color="district"))
    + geom_line(size=1)
    + geom_point(size=1.8)
    + geom_line(cambridge, size=2.4)  # emphasize the focus district
    + scale_color_manual(
        values=cc.PALETTE, name="", limits=cc.district_order(cc.GRAPH_GROUP)
    )
    + scale_x_continuous(breaks=list(range(2015, 2025)))
    + labs(
        x="School year ending",
        y="Full-value tax rate (per $1,000)",
        title="Full-value property tax rate, 2014–15 to 2023–24",
    )
    + cc.theme_ccs()
)








Figure 5.2: Full-value tax rate by school year ending, 2015 (2014–15) through 2024 (2023–24). The rate is the levy per $1,000 of taxable full value. Source: NYS Comptroller local-government data (Table 3).
Cambridge’s tax rate has fluctuated. It held around $16–$17 per $1,000 of full value for most of the decade, then dropped sharply in 2022–23 and 2023–24 — to about $14.8 and $13.8 respectively. title: 5  Property taxes
text:
5.2 What this means for Cambridge
The tax story has two parts:

The levy (what Cambridge collects) has risen 17% over the decade, to $9.6 million. This funds the district’s operations — the same spending per pupil discussed in the Spending chapter.
The rate (what property owners pay) has fallen recently due to a reassessment, which could provide relief to property owners even as the levy continues to climb.

The underlying pressure is the same: Cambridge’s declining enrollment (see the Enrollment chapter) means fewer taxpayers to spread the levy across, even as fixed costs remain. The district’s low students-per-teacher ratio (see the Class size chapter) adds spending pressure, which ultimately feeds back into what the levy must collect.
Data sources: - NYS Comptroller school-district tax levies and full-value rates, by district × town (2015–2024) - NYSED Property Tax Report Card — proposed budgets, levy limits, and projected enrollment
This chapter is new; there is no exploratory version yet. For detailed property-tax analysis across towns and years, see the data in data/processed/osc_school_district_tax_levies.parquet.

title: 6  The property-tax cap
text:
6.1 How the cap works
New York’s property-tax cap is the most important single rule governing how fast a school district’s tax levy — the total property tax it collects — can grow. This chapter uses NYSED’s Property Tax Report Card, the figures each district files in spring showing the budget it puts before voters, to see how much room Cambridge has under the cap and how the cap binds districts across the state.
It pairs with two other chapters: Taxes, which covers the levy and tax rate Cambridge actually collected; and Contingency budgets, which covers what happens when a budget vote fails.
In plain terms: a district may raise its tax levy by the lesser of 2% or the inflation rate (CPI) without any special action. To grow the levy faster than that, the budget must win a 60% supermajority at the polls — not the usual simple majority (50% + 1). Each district’s allowable growth for the year, after the state’s formula, is its levy limit.
There is an important escape valve. Certain costs — growth in the pension contribution, spending on capital projects, and some court-ordered payments — can be excluded from the cap. So a district whose proposed levy is above its base levy limit may still be within the cap once these permissible exclusions are applied. Only the amount over the limit after exclusions triggers the 60% requirement. (This is why the Contingency budgets chapter relies on NYSED’s vote-file 60%-flag for actual override outcomes, rather than on a levy-vs-limit calculation alone.)

title: 6  The property-tax cap
text:
6.5 A note on reserves
A district facing the cap can draw on fund balance — its reserves — to ease the squeeze. Cambridge reports an unrestricted fund balance of about 3–4% of its budget across these years, and in some years uses a tax-cap reserve. The role of reserves and fund balance in Cambridge’s finances is taken up in the reserves chapter.
Source: NYSED Property Tax Report Card, via data/processed/property_tax_report_card.parquet. Year = school-year-ending (2027 = 2026–27). The over/under gap is computed as proposed levy − levy limit (both excluding permissible exclusions); the stored levy_vs_limit field is not used because NYSED flips its sign at 2016 (see the Sources appendix).

title: 7  Reserves and fund balance
text:
7.1 Two kinds of “reserves”: reserve funds and fund balance
A school district’s reserves and fund balance are its savings — money set aside from prior years that is not yet spent. They are a buffer for cash-flow gaps, a cushion against unexpected costs, and a way to save for planned future expenses. The size of a district’s reserves is also one of the clearest signs of its fiscal health: too little leaves it exposed; the state limits how much of the flexible kind a district may hold.
This chapter explains the different kinds of reserves a New York school district keeps, the legal cap on the flexible part, how districts across the state sit relative to that cap, and how Cambridge’s own reserves have changed. It draws on the official chart of accounts (kept in this repo’s docs/ folder), NYSED’s Property Tax Report Card, OSC school-district finance data, and Cambridge’s audited annual financial reports.
The word “reserves” gets used two ways, and the distinction matters.
Reserve funds are accounts created under specific state laws, each for a specific purpose. The money in them is restricted — it can only be used for that purpose. A New York school district’s main reserve funds are:
Fund balance is the broader accounting term for the net resources of a fund after liabilities. The General Fund’s fund balance is classified into:
So the relationship is: reserve funds are the restricted portion of fund balance. A district can hold large amounts in restricted reserves and still have little unassigned balance — and it is the unassigned balance that the law caps.

Restricted — the reserve funds above (each legally earmarked).
Committed — set aside by board action for a specific purpose.
Assigned — intended for a particular use (e.g., appropriated into the next year’s budget, or unappropriated).
Unassigned — the residual, general-purpose balance with no specific designation. This is the flexible “rainy-day” money.

title: 7  Reserves and fund balance
text:
7.2 The 4% cap
New York’s property-tax-cap law (Real Property Tax Law §2-c / Education Law §2023-a) limits a school district’s unassigned (unexpended-surplus) fund balance to 4% of the prior year’s budget. This is the measure NYSED reports in the Property Tax Report Card as fund_balance_unrestricted_pct_of_budget.
The cap applies only to the unassigned balance, not to the restricted reserve funds. That is why a district with substantial capital, retirement, or tax- certiorari reserves is not “over the cap” — those are restricted for their purposes. The 4% rule constrains only the flexible money a district can keep on hand.1

title: 7  Reserves and fund balance
text:
7.3 Why districts build specific reserves
Each reserve fund exists to set aside money for a specific, often lumpy or unpredictable cost the district is legally required to pay — smoothing a bill that would otherwise hit the budget all at once. The clearest example is the retirement reserve. A district’s pension cost depends on an annually-set employer contribution rate that swings widely: NYSTRS’s teachers’-system rate has ranged from roughly 6% to 17.5% of payroll since 2014-15 (it was 16.25% for 2023-24, for example).2 By contributing to the reserve in lower-rate years, a district builds a cushion it can draw on when the rate spikes — instead of cutting programs or sharply raising taxes that year. The same logic runs through the other reserves: each earmarks savings for a cost that is mandatory, large, and hard to time — tax-refund judgments, insurance or workers’-comp claims, capital projects.
A second reason matters under the tax cap: restricted reserve funds are not subject to the 4% unassigned-fund-balance limit. Building reserves is therefore the main way a district holds savings beyond that 4% flexible ceiling — the trade-off being that the money is locked to its specific purpose.

title: 7  Reserves and fund balance
text:
7.4 Using reserves for budget relief
A district spends down a reserve by appropriating it — booking a planned draw as a revenue source in the coming year’s budget. When, say, $X is appropriated from the TRS reserve toward that year’s pension bill, the budget needs $X less from taxes and state aid for the TRS line, freeing room for other spending, for tax relief, or to rebuild other reserves. Cambridge’s board did this for 2025-26, appropriating $625,000 from the capital reserve and $200,119 from the employee-benefit reserve.
Two points keep this honest:

It is not new money. The reserve was funded from prior years’ taxes, so drawing it shifts cost backward and leaves less cushion for the next shock — it smooths timing; it does not create resources.
It works within the cap. Using reserves as a revenue source lowers the levy the budget requires — a key lever for staying under the tax cap, though leaning on it too hard erodes the buffer.

Reserve funds can be spent only for their statutory purpose — a TRS reserve cannot buy a roof, and a capital reserve cannot pay a pension bill. Several carry extra rules (from the chart of accounts and Cambridge’s audited reports):

Capital reserve (Education Law §3651): both establishing it and every expenditure from it require voter approval by proposition.
Tax certiorari reserve (General Municipal Law §3651.1-a): mandatory to fund; the balance may not exceed anticipated judgments and claims, and any unused amount must be returned to the General Fund by the first day of the fourth fiscal year after deposit. (Cambridge was flagged in 2024-25 for over-funding this reserve; it was corrected.)
Retirement reserve (General Municipal Law §6-r): a board may contribute up to 2% of covered salaries per year, with the TRS sub-fund capped at 10% of covered salaries.
Employee benefit reserve (General Municipal Law §6-p): established by board vote; for accrued benefits payable on termination.

The unassigned balance, by contrast, has no purpose restriction — it is the flexible money — which is precisely why the state caps it at 4%.

title: 7  Reserves and fund balance
section: 7.7 Cambridge’s reserves by type, over time
text:
7.7 Cambridge’s reserves by type, over time


Code
fin = pl.read_parquet(CAM_FINANCE_PATH)
years = list(range(2013, 2026))


def _amt(code: str, year: int):
    """End-of-year balance ($, or None) for one account code in one year."""
    s = fin.filter((pl.col("ACCOUNT_CODE") == code) & (pl.col("CALENDAR_YEAR") == year)).select("AMOUNT").to_series()
    return s.first() if s.len() else None


rows = []
for code, label in RESERVE_CODES.items():
    rows.append({"Reserve": label, **{str(y): (_amt(code, y) or 0) / 1000 for y in years}})

res_df = pl.DataFrame(rows)
res_df = pl.concat([
    res_df,
    pl.DataFrame([{"Reserve": "Total restricted reserves",
                   **{str(y): sum(filter(None, (_amt(c, y) for c in RESERVE_CODES))) / 1000
                      for y in years}}]),
    pl.DataFrame([{"Reserve": "Unassigned (4%-capped)",
                   **{str(y): (_amt("A917", y) or 0) / 1000 for y in years}}]),
    pl.DataFrame([{"Reserve": "Total General Fund balance",
                   **{str(y): (_amt("A8029", y) or 0) / 1000 for y in years}}]),
], how="vertical")

year_cols = [str(y) for y in years]
bold_rows = pl.col("Reserve").is_in(["Total restricted reserves", "Total General Fund balance"])

The reports also note the reserves the board has appropriated (budgeted to spend) for 2025–26: $625,000 from the capital reserve and $200,119 from the employee-benefit reserve.
The audits flagged two reserve-related items, both since corrected:

Tax-certiorari reserve over-funding (finding 2024-002). The reserve exceeded the amount of anticipated tax-certiorari claims. General Municipal Law §3651.1-a caps this reserve at anticipated judgments and refunds, and any unused balance must be returned to the General Fund. Cambridge corrected the over-funding.
A capital-assets / net-debt presentation issue (finding 2024-001, also corrected), where invested-in-capital-assets-net-of-related-debt appeared negative.

Taken together, Cambridge’s reserves are healthy and growing, concentrated in restricted funds tied to specific future costs (capital projects, pensions), while its flexible unassigned balance is held at the legal 4% ceiling.

title: 8  Contingency budgets
text:
8.1 How often budgets fail
Every May, each New York school district puts its proposed budget to a public vote. If a budget is defeated — fewer than half of voters say yes — the district gets a second chance: it may hold a re-vote in June. If the budget fails again, or the district decides not to try again, the district must operate the next year on a contingency budget — a pared-back spending plan set by state rules, with no new programs and tight limits on spending growth.
One rule shapes most of what follows. New York’s property-tax cap says a district may raise its tax levy by only a small amount each year without special permission. A district that wants to go above the cap must win a 60% supermajority at the polls. A budget at or below the cap needs only a simple majority (more than 50%). That higher, 60% bar is why above-cap budgets fail more often — and why the tax cap and contingency budgets are linked.
This chapter uses NYSED’s statewide School District Budget Voting Results to count, year by year, how many districts defeat a budget, hold a re-vote, and end up on a contingency budget — and where Cambridge and its neighbors fit in.
Budget defeats are rare, and most districts that fail once recover. Across the 14 votes from 2012 through 2025, only 198 of roughly 9,400 district-votes ended in a first-vote defeat — about two per 100. Of those 198 defeats, 125 districts (63%) passed their budget on the June re-vote, and 73 (37%) went on to a contingency budget. Defeats cluster in single digits most years, with 2025 (24 defeats) the highest since 2013.
Code
yearly = (
    panel.group_by("year")
    .agg(
        pl.len().alias("Failed first vote"),
        pl.col("outcome").str.starts_with("Contingency").sum().alias("On contingency"),
    )
    .sort("year")
    .unpivot(index="year", variable_name="measure", value_name="count")
)

(
    ggplot(yearly, aes("year", "count", fill="measure"))
    + geom_col(position=position_dodge(width=0.75), width=0.7)
    + scale_fill_manual(
        values={"Failed first vote": "#C8102E", "On contingency": "#2C6E9B"}, name=""
    )
    + scale_x_continuous(breaks=list(range(2012, 2026)))
    + scale_y_continuous(breaks=list(range(0, 36, 5)))
    + labs(
        x="Vote year (the May vote)",
        y="Number of districts",
        title="Budget defeats and contingency budgets, 2012–2025",
    )
    + cc.theme_ccs()
    + theme(figure_size=(9, 4.5))
)








Figure 8.1: First-vote budget defeats and contingency budgets by vote year, 2012–2025. A ‘defeat’ is a budget that failed the May vote; ‘on contingency’ is the smaller subset that did not pass a June re-vote. Source: NYSED statewide budget voting results.
The gap between the two bars is the share of defeated districts that recovered on a re-vote. Contingency budgets — the red bar’s smaller companion — are consistently a handful of districts each year. 2020 stands out as unusual: votes were conducted entirely by absentee ballot during the pandemic, with a shifted June re-vote date, so its pattern (12 of 12 defeated districts ending on contingency) is not comparable to other years.

title: 8  Contingency budgets
text:
8.3 The 60% threshold
The tax cap’s 60% supermajority rule is the mechanism behind most above-cap defeats. The table below follows the 198 districts that failed a first vote and asks, for their second try: was the budget they put up above the cap (needing 60%) or at or below the cap (needing only a simple majority), and did it pass or send the district to contingency?


Code
# Cap status of the budget governing the second event: the re-vote's flag if a
# re-vote was held, else the first vote's flag. Null where no 60% flag was
# reported (older files).
cap_outcome = (
    panel.with_columns(pl.coalesce(["above_cap2", "above_cap"]).alias("cap2"))
    .with_columns(
        pl.when(pl.col("cap2").is_null())
        .then(pl.lit("No 60% flag in file"))
        .when(pl.col("cap2"))
        .then(pl.lit("Above cap — 60% required"))
        .otherwise(pl.lit("At/below cap — simple majority"))
        .alias("cap_status")
    )
    .group_by("cap_status", "outcome")
    .len()
    .pivot("outcome", index="cap_status", values="len", aggregate_function="sum")
    .fill_null(0)
)

# Stable row order and tidy outcome columns.
order = ["Above cap — 60% required", "At/below cap — simple majority", "No 60% flag in file"]
cap_outcome = (
    cap_outcome.with_columns(pl.col("cap_status").replace_strict({k: i for i, k in enumerate(order)}).alias("_o"))
    .sort("_o")
    .drop("_o")
)
for c in ["Passed on revote", "Contingency (failed revote)", "Contingency (no revote)"]:
    if c not in cap_outcome.columns:
        cap_outcome = cap_outcome.with_columns(pl.lit(0, dtype=pl.Int64).alias(c))
cap_outcome = cap_outcome.select("cap_status", "Passed on revote",
                                 "Contingency (failed revote)", "Contingency (no revote)")

(
    GT(cap_outcome, rowname_col="cap_status")
    .tab_header(
        title="Second-vote outcomes by tax-cap status",
        subtitle="Districts that failed the first vote, 2012–2025",
    )
    .tab_spanner(label="Passed", columns=["Passed on revote"])
    .tab_spanner(label="Went to contingency", columns=["Contingency (failed revote)", "Contingency (no revote)"])
    .tab_source_note(
        md(
            "**Source:** NYSED statewide budget voting results (computed). Cap "
            "status comes from NYSED's 60%-required flag, the only cap signal these "
            "files carry (they include no levy figures). 'No 60% flag in file' covers "
            "older files that did not report the flag."

Two Washington County neighbors have not been so steady:

Salem failed its first vote in May 2024 — about 59% yes, but the budget was above the cap, so it needed 60%. Salem then reduced the budget below the cap for the June re-vote, where 71% voted yes, and passed.

No other Washington County district in this book’s comparison group (Greenwich, Granville, Hudson Falls, Whitehall, and the rest) defeated a budget in any year on record.

title: 8  Contingency budgets
text:
8.6 Notes on the data

What “year” means. This chapter indexes by the vote’s calendar year (the May 2024 vote is “2024”). The budget adopted at that vote funds the following school year (the May 2024 vote adopts the 2024–25 budget). That is one off from the school-year-ending label used elsewhere in this book.
No levy figures. NYSED’s vote files carry no tax-levy data — only a “60% required” flag marking budgets above the cap. Cap status here comes entirely from that flag; the actual levy percentages live in a different source (the OSC / tax report-card data used in the Taxes chapter).
Re-vote linking is best-effort. A first vote and its re-vote are matched by district name within the year. NYSED spells district names inconsistently across files (and used all-caps in the older files), so a handful of re-votes each year do not link to a first-vote record; counts of re-vote outcomes are therefore approximate for the older years and reliable from about 2020 on.
Pre-2012. The tax cap took effect in 2012, so the above/below-cap split is undefined before then; earlier years are omitted.
Validation. The computed first-vote defeat counts match NYSED’s own published statewide totals exactly for 2021–2025 (and within one for the anomalous 2020).

Data pipeline: src/download_budget_votes.py fetches the workbooks; src/build_budget_votes.py parses them into data/processed/budget_votes.parquet. See the data sources appendix.

title: 9  Student test results
text:
9.1 Why scores don’t compare across all years
On New York’s grades 3–8 English Language Arts (ELA) and Mathematics tests, Cambridge’s proficiency rates — the share of students scoring at or above grade level — are generally in the middle of nearby districts. In 2023–24 (the most recent pre-Next-Generation year with normal participation), about 46% of Cambridge students tested proficient in ELA and 49% in Math, compared to statewide averages of roughly 48% and 51% respectively.
These results come from New York’s Report Card Database, which reports test results for every district. The chart below shows how Cambridge’s ELA proficiency rate has changed since 2015–16, alongside nearby districts for context.
Cambridge’s ELA proficiency rate has fluctuated, as have all districts’. The sharp dip in 2020–21 reflects pandemic disruption — only about half of students participated that year, so the rate is not representative. Tests were cancelled entirely in spring 2020.
The Math proficiency picture is similar:
Math proficiency rates show more year-to-year volatility than ELA, which is typical. Cambridge’s rates are generally middle of the pack — below Schuylerville and often Greenwich in both subjects, but above Salem in many years.
New York has redesigned the tests and changed the passing standards twice in this 10-year window. As a result, proficiency rates are comparable within three eras, but not across them:
A change in the proficiency rate across one of these boundaries reflects the new standard as much as any change in student learning. For example, the 2017→2018 jump for many districts is largely due to the redesign, not a sudden improvement in student skills.
2020–21 is an outlier: Tests were cancelled in spring 2020. In 2021, only about half of students participated (many families opted out), so the 2021 rates are not representative and should be read with caution.

text:
2015–2017 (Common Core era): Tests under the Common Core standards
2018–2022 (Redesign): New tests and new passing standards adopted in 2018
2023–2025 (Next Generation): New tests aligned to the Next Generation Learning Standards

title: 9  Student test results
text:
9.3 What this means
Test scores are one measure of how a school system is serving its students. Cambridge’s scores are generally middle of the pack — not the highest, not the lowest, and tracking with regional trends. The district’s smaller class size and lower students-per-teacher ratio (see the Class size chapter) don’t show up as dramatically higher test scores, which is consistent with national research: smaller classes help, but many other factors shape student achievement.
The enrollment decline documented in the Enrollment chapter doesn’t appear to have hurt outcomes — Cambridge’s scores have held steady while the student body shrank. That’s a positive sign for the district’s educational quality.
For additional subgroup breakdowns, grade-level detail, or comparison to statewide benchmarks, see the full panel in data/processed/assessments_em_ela_math.parquet. The exploratory versions of these chapters, when developed, will provide deeper analysis.
Data source: NYSED Report Card Database, ten years (2015–16 through 2024–25), stitched into one district-by-year panel. Raw data: data/raw/nysed_report_card/zips/ (SRC{YEAR}.zip, ~3.3 GB total, git-ignored).

title: 10  Is merger an option?
text:
10.1 What “merger” means in New York
Cambridge, like many small rural districts in New York, is shrinking. Enrollment is falling and the population is aging (see Demographics), which puts steady pressure on spending, taxes, and reserves (see those chapters). One structural response New York explicitly allows is merger — combining two or more school districts into one. This chapter explains what merger means in New York, the financial incentive the state offers for it, what has actually happened when New York districts have merged, and where Cambridge sits among its neighbors.
Whether merger is right for Cambridge is a decision for the community and its school board. What follows is the mechanics and the evidence.
New York’s term is reorganization — the act of creating a new school district where one or more existed before. State Education Law §314 keeps a running “Master Plan for School District Reorganization” (first drawn up in 1942) so district boundaries can change as communities’ needs and tax bases shift.
There are three main forms:
A district can also “tuition” students — contract with a neighbor to educate a grade level — but that is not a merger: no new district is formed, and no reorganization incentive aid is paid.

title: 10  Is merger an option?
text:
Centralization — the merging districts dissolve and a new school district is created covering all of their territory. (This is what most people mean by “merger.”)
Annexation — one district dissolves and is absorbed into a neighboring Central or Union Free district; no new district is created.
Annexation by a city school district — a district outside a city is absorbed into the city district, which becomes an “Enlarged City School District.”

title: 10  Is merger an option?
text:
10.2 How a merger happens
The process takes roughly a year and has several built-in public checkpoints (NYSED, School District Reorganization: A Primer, July 2024):

The boards of the affected districts hold joint meetings and commission a feasibility study (enrollment, staffing, programs, transportation, and the fiscal picture — state aid, spending, and the local tax impact). A state Local Government Efficiency Grant can offset the study’s cost.
The boards hold public information meetings.
Each district holds a non-binding “straw vote” to gauge support.
The BOCES District Superintendent reports the straw-vote result to the Commissioner of Education; a letter from the Superintendent in support starts the formal (statutory) process.
The Commissioner issues an order; the statutory vote follows; the earliest a new district can begin operating is July 1.

title: 10  Is merger an option?
text:
10.3 Two routes, two vote thresholds
The single most important practical difference between centralization and annexation is how the vote works:

Centralization requires eligible voters in each district to petition for a vote, and a majority in favor at every voting location is needed to form the new district. In other words, both communities must say yes.
Annexation is different: the Commissioner’s order is final after 60 days unless residents petition for a vote. A vote is not legally required, and a majority in favor at any one voting district finalizes the annexation.

So annexation is the lower-bar path — it can be carried by a single district’s yes vote, or even take effect without a vote. That asymmetry shapes which route real merger discussions tend to take.

title: 10  Is merger an option?
text:
10.4 The financial incentive
The state pushes districts toward merger with a large but temporary aid package:

Reorganization Incentive Operating Aid (RIOA) pays the merged district an extra 40% of its combined Foundation Aid for the first five years, then drops by four percentage points a year, ending after 14 years. Since 2024-25, that percentage is applied to the merged district’s Foundation Aid at the effective date of the merger; before that it used a frozen 2006-07 base, so the 2024 change roughly tripled the incentive for a district merging today. (A long-dormant statutory limit can reduce the amount for some districts; NYSED advises districts to check.)
Reorganization Incentive Building Aid lifts a district’s building-aid ratio by 30 percentage points (capped at 95%) for new or renovated space, available for 10 years.

For scale, here is what the 14-year RIOA stream looks like applied to Cambridge’s own Foundation Aid ($9.55 million in 2025-26) as an illustrative base. The real amount would be 40% of the combined Foundation Aid of whatever districts merged, so this is a lower bound for a Cambridge-centered merger.


Code
(
    ggplot(rioa.with_columns((pl.col("aid") / 1e6).alias("aid_m")), aes("year", "aid_m"))
    + geom_col(fill="#C8102E", width=0.7)
    + geom_text(aes(label="pct"), va="bottom", format_string="{}%", size=8, color="#444444")
    + scale_x_continuous(breaks=list(range(1, 15)))
    + scale_y_continuous(limits=(0, 4.6), breaks=list(range(0, 5)),
                         labels=lambda lst: [f"${v}M" for v in lst])
    + labs(
        x="Year after merger", y="Incentive aid (illustrative)",
        title="Reorganization incentive aid, 14-year schedule",
        subtitle=f"Applied to Cambridge's Foundation Aid; total ≈ ${rioa_total/1e6:,.1f}M over 14 years",
    )
    + cc.theme_ccs()
    + theme(figure_size=(8, 4.2))
)








Figure 10.1: Reorganization Incentive Operating Aid over the 14-year eligibility period, shown applied to Cambridge’s 2025-26 Foundation Aid ($9.55M) as an illustrative base. The merged district receives 40% of its combined Foundation Aid for five years, then four percentage points less each year. A real Cambridge-centered merger would be calculated on the larger combined base of the merging districts. Source: NYSED Reorganization Primer (2024); RIOA schedule per Education Law §3602.





Two real New York mergers show what that money — and its limits — look like in practice (NYSSBA On Board, April 2025):

Central Valley CSD (Ilion + Mohawk, Herkimer County, 2013) is on track to receive about $42 million in incentive aid over its 14 years. It used the money to expand offerings (16 AP courses plus 25-30 college-credit courses), add sports and extracurriculars, bolster reserves, and hold down the tax levy. But it is now in year 12 of 14 — the incentive is almost gone, and district leaders describe hard decisions ahead. (Central Valley got 73.5% of its funding from state aid in 2022-23.)
Boquet Valley CSD (Elizabethtown-Lewis + Westport, Essex County, 2019) receives $6 million over 15 years under the old formula — it would have gotten $18 million under the current one. It expanded electives and AP options, but still operates in two aging buildings; a capital project to consolidate onto one campus was defeated by voters, and the community still wrangles over the school’s name and colors.

A retired BOCES district superintendent, Robert Guiffreda, has suggested merged districts split incentive aid roughly into thirds — tax relief, enhanced programming, and capital needs (more if a new building is required).

title: 10  Is merger an option?
text:
10.5 Mergers are rare
For all the incentive, very few New York districts actually merge. The two above are among the only centralizations in recent decades. Far more common are studies that go nowhere: roughly 30 districts have studied merger since 2010 without implementing one (per the state school business officials’ association), and failed votes dot the record — Scio and Wellsville (2010), Lake Pleasant and Wells (2011). Loss of local identity — team nicknames, mascots, rivalries — is one of the most frequently cited reasons a merger vote fails.

title: 10  Is merger an option?
text:
10.6 What the research says
The broader research on district consolidation (e.g., a review of the research on district and school consolidation) points in a consistent direction on money, a less settled one on academics:

Cost savings are real but concentrated among the smallest districts. Combining two small districts can lower per-pupil costs meaningfully; the savings shrink as the districts involved get larger, and can be eaten back by longer bus rides and higher transportation costs.
Academic effects are mixed. Larger districts can offer more courses and programs (as Central Valley and Boquet Valley found), but evidence that consolidation by itself raises student achievement is weak.
The trade-offs are local: longer travel times, less local control, staff disruption, and the loss of a community institution.

In short, the financial case is strongest precisely for the kind of small rural districts that surround Cambridge.

title: 10  Is merger an option?
text:
10.7 Where Cambridge stands
Cambridge is a mid-size district in a county of small ones. The table below places it among its Washington County neighbors (plus Schuylerville and Hoosick Falls, just across the county line). Cambridge’s 741 students and $9.5 million in Foundation Aid sit in the middle of the pack; five neighbors are smaller by enrollment (Hartford, Fort Edward, Fort Ann, Argyle, Salem) — and the research above finds consolidation’s per-pupil savings are largest precisely when the districts combining are small.


Code
group = {**cc.WASHINGTON_K12, "52170104": "Schuylerville", "49050106": "Hoosick Falls"}

enr = (cc.load_panel()
       .filter((pl.col("district_cd").is_in(list(group))) & (pl.col("year_end") == 2025))
       .select(pl.col("district_cd").replace_strict(group).alias("district"),
               "district_cd", "k12_enrollment"))

fa = (state_aid
      .filter((pl.col("aid_category") == "Foundation Aid") & (pl.col("year_end") == 2026))
      .select(pl.col("nysed_district_cd").alias("district_cd"),
              pl.col("amount").alias("foundation_aid")))

tbl = (enr.join(fa, on="district_cd", how="left")
       .with_columns((pl.col("foundation_aid") / pl.col("k12_enrollment")).alias("fa_per_pupil"))
       .sort("k12_enrollment", descending=True)
       .select("district", "k12_enrollment", "foundation_aid", "fa_per_pupil"))

(
    GT(tbl, rowname_col="district")
    .tab_header(title="Cambridge among its neighbors",
                subtitle="2024-25 enrollment and 2025-26 Foundation Aid")
    .fmt_number(columns="k12_enrollment", decimals=0)
    .fmt_currency(columns=["foundation_aid", "fa_per_pupil"], decimals=0)
    .cols_label(k12_enrollment="K-12 enrollment",
                foundation_aid="Foundation Aid",
                fa_per_pupil="Foundation Aid per pupil")
    .tab_style(
        style=[style.fill(color="#FCEFB4"), style.text(weight="bold")],
        locations=[loc.body(rows=pl.col("district") == "Cambridge"),
                   loc.stub(rows=pl.col("district") == "Cambridge")],
    )
    .tab_source_note(md("**Source:** NYSED Enrollment database (2024-25 K-12 enrollment); "
                        "NYS state aid (data.ny.gov, 2025-26 enacted Foundation Aid)."))

title: 11  Regionalization
text:
What is it?
Can it help?

title: Appendix A — Students per teacher
text:
A.1 Average class size
A natural question is how crowded Cambridge’s classrooms are compared with nearby districts. The measure most people picture is class size — the number of students in a typical classroom. NYSED does publish an average class size for each district, and we look at it first; but it is reported only for a handful of specific grades and subjects (there is no single official number) and only since 2018–19. So for a longer, more complete view we also use K-12 students per teacher: a district’s K-12 enrollment divided by its number of teachers.
Students per teacher is a useful staffing comparison, but it is not class size, and it is always lower than class size — because it counts every teacher (including part-time teachers and specialists who don’t lead a regular class), not just the teachers standing in front of a full classroom. Read it as a staffing ratio, not a count of children in a room.
A reminder from the Overview: the teacher figure is a district-wide headcount (all grades, not full-time-equivalents), so this is an approximate measure for comparing districts and tracking change over time. Lower means fewer students per teacher.
Start with the measure people actually picture. NYSED reports an average class size for each district — but only for a specific set of classes (the early elementary grades and the courses tied to State tests), and only since 2018–19. There is no single official “district class size,” so the line below is the median across all of a district’s reported classes each year: a robust middle value that a stray two-student elective can’t throw off.
Cambridge has the smallest typical class size of the group.
Code
g = cc.class_size_median_for(cc.GRAPH_GROUP).drop_nulls("median_class_size")
cambridge = g.filter(pl.col("district") == cc.FOCUS)

(
    ggplot(g, aes("year_end", "median_class_size", color="district"))
    + geom_line(size=1)
    + geom_point(size=1.8)
    + geom_line(cambridge, size=2.4)  # emphasize the focus district
    + scale_color_manual(
        values=cc.PALETTE, name="", limits=cc.district_order(cc.GRAPH_GROUP)
    )
    + scale_x_continuous(breaks=list(range(2019, 2026)))
    + labs(
        x="School year ending",
        y="Median class size",
        # Title states only the facts; the interpretive headline lives above the
        # figure in the prose (see the project's graph-title convention).
        title="Class size",
    )
    + cc.theme_ccs()
)








Figure A.1: Median of NYSED’s reported average class sizes across all measured classes (early elementary grades and courses aligned to State tests), by district and school year ending, 2019 (2018–19) through 2025 (2024–25). NYSED publishes class size only for these specific classes and only since 2018–19; the median summarizes them and resists small-section outliers. The 2020–21 (2021) figures are affected by pandemic reporting disruption — Salem’s dip that year is a data artifact, not a real change. Source: NYSED Student & Educator (Average Class Size) database.
Across these years Cambridge’s median class size runs about 11 to 15 students — consistently the lowest or near-lowest of the five, and several students below Schuylerville, Salem and Hoosick Falls. (Salem’s sharp dip in 2021 is a pandemic-year reporting artifact, not a real change.) That is the same story the staffing ratio tells next, seen at the level of actual classrooms: smaller classes, fewer students per teacher.
Two cautions before leaning on it too hard. NYSED measures class size for only a handful of classes, and the counts are small — a single combined section or a lightly-enrolled elective can swing a small district’s numbers from year to year. And the series only starts in 2018–19. For a longer, more complete view we turn to students per teacher — which exists for every district back to 2017–18, though, as the next section explains, it is a staffing ratio, not class size.

title: Appendix A — Students per teacher

A.1.1 A sharper cut: grades 3–8 core classes

A cleaner, apples-to-apples comparison restricts to the twelve core academic classes every district teaches and reports: English and Math in grades 3 through 8. (NYSED relabeled these classes in 2021; the build unifies the old and new names.)
On the same twelve classes, Cambridge’s are the smallest in the group — by several students per class.

The chart below tracks the graph group from school-year-ending 2018 (the first year teacher counts exist) through 2025.
Cambridge now has the fewest students per teacher of the group.

    + labs(

Figure A.3: K–12 students per teacher by school year ending, 2018 (2017–18) through 2025 (2024–25). Lower means fewer students per teacher. Source: NYSED Enrollment and Student & Educator databases.





In 2017–18 only Salem had a lower ratio than Cambridge. Cambridge’s ratio then drifted down — its K-12 enrollment fell about 15% (from 869 to 741) while its teacher headcount stayed roughly flat — and by the two most recent years Cambridge has the lowest students-per-teacher ratio of the five. A jump in reported teachers in 2023–24 pushed its 2024 figure especially low (8.2). Schuylerville and Greenwich actually fell more over the full period (about 2.0 and 1.9, versus Cambridge’s 1.5), but from much higher starting points, so they remain above Cambridge.

title: Appendix A — Students per teacher
section: A.3 Another view: teachers per 100 students
text:
A.3 Another view: teachers per 100 students
Students per teacher and teachers per 100 students are the very same data seen from opposite ends. The first asks how many students share each teacher; the second asks how many teachers a district employs for every 100 students. One is just the other turned upside-down — but flipping it puts staffing, rather than crowding, in the foreground, and now a higher line means more teachers per student.
Seen this way, Cambridge now employs the most teachers per 100 students of the group.


Code
g = (
    cc.panel_for(cc.GRAPH_GROUP)  # 5 districts, 2018+
    .select("district", "year_end", "num_teachers", "k12_enrollment")
    .drop_nulls()
    .with_columns(
        (pl.col("num_teachers") / pl.col("k12_enrollment") * 100).alias(
            "teachers_per_100_students"
        )
    )
)
cambridge = g.filter(pl.col("district") == cc.FOCUS)

(
    ggplot(g, aes("year_end", "teachers_per_100_students", color="district"))
    + geom_line(size=1)
    + geom_point(size=1.8)
    + geom_line(cambridge, size=2.4)  # emphasize the focus district
    + scale_color_manual(
        values=cc.PALETTE, name="", limits=cc.district_order(cc.GRAPH_GROUP)
    )
    + scale_x_continuous(breaks=list(range(2018, 2026)))
    + labs(
        x="School year ending",
        y="K–12 teachers per 100 students",
        # Title states only the facts; the interpretive headline lives above the
        # figure in the prose (see the project's graph-title convention).
        title="Teachers per 100 students",
    )
    + cc.theme_ccs()
)








Figure A.4: K–12 teachers per 100 students by school year ending, 2018 (2017–18) through 2025 (2024–25). Higher means more teachers per student. Computed as a district’s teacher headcount ÷ its K-12 enrollment × 100. Source: NYSED Enrollment and Student & Educator databases.





This is the mirror image of the first chart: wherever a district’s students-per-teacher ratio fell, its teachers-per-100-students rose. In 2017–18 only Salem employed more teachers per 100 students than Cambridge. Cambridge’s line then climbed — its K-12 enrollment fell about 15% while its teacher headcount held roughly steady — and by the two most recent years Cambridge has the most teachers per 100 students of the five (about 11.5 in 2024–25). The jump in reported teachers in 2023–24 pushed its 2024 figure especially high (12.2). The two larger districts, Schuylerville and Hoosick Falls, stay lowest, around 9 to 9.6 teachers per 100 students.
The table below puts the two ends of Cambridge’s story side by side, and shows the arithmetic behind the rise.
Cambridge’s enrollment fell about 15% while its teacher headcount held steady, so teachers per 100 students rose about 17%.

title: Appendix A — Students per teacher
section: A.5 A national cross-check: NCES teacher FTE
text:
A.5 A national cross-check: NCES teacher FTE
The ratio above has two limitations worth testing. NYSED reports a teacher headcount, whereas the standard pupil-teacher ratio — the one NCES, the OECD and most cross-state comparisons use — divides enrollment by teacher full-time-equivalents (FTE). And it comes from a single state source. The federal Common Core of Data (CCD) offers an independent check: it reports teacher FTE and enrollment for every district. (CCD labels a school year by its fall term, so its years are shifted here to match NYSED’s school-year-ending labels.)
First the diagnostic question — do the two measures agree for Cambridge?
The two measures never differ by more than about two-thirds of a student per teacher, and both fall sharply in 2023–24.


Code
ny = (
    cc.panel_for(cc.GRAPH_GROUP)
    .filter(pl.col("district") == cc.FOCUS)
    .select("year_end", pl.col("k12_students_per_teacher").alias("ratio"))
    .drop_nulls()
    .with_columns(pl.lit("NYSED (teacher headcount)").alias("source"))
)
ccd_cam = (
    cc.ccd_ratio_for(cc.GRAPH_GROUP)
    .filter((pl.col("district") == cc.FOCUS) & (pl.col("year_end") >= 2018))
    .select("year_end", pl.col("ccd_students_per_teacher").alias("ratio"))
    .drop_nulls()
    .with_columns(pl.lit("NCES (teacher FTE)").alias("source"))
)
both = pl.concat(
    [
        ny.with_columns(pl.col("year_end").cast(pl.Int64)),
        ccd_cam.with_columns(pl.col("year_end").cast(pl.Int64)),
    ]
)
source_colors = {
    "NYSED (teacher headcount)": "#C8102E",
    "NCES (teacher FTE)": "#2C6E9B",
}

(
    ggplot(both, aes("year_end", "ratio", color="source"))
    + geom_line(size=1.2)
    + geom_point(size=2)
    + scale_color_manual(values=source_colors, name="")
    + scale_x_continuous(breaks=list(range(2018, 2026)))
    + labs(
        x="School year ending",
        y="Students per teacher",
        title="Cambridge students per teacher: headcount vs. FTE",
    )
    + cc.theme_ccs()
)








Figure A.5: Cambridge students per teacher: NYSED (teacher headcount) vs. NCES Common Core of Data (teacher FTE), by school year ending. Sources: NYSED Enrollment and Student & Educator databases; NCES CCD via the Urban Institute Education Data Portal.





The two lines stay close because, in small districts, a teacher headcount and its full-time-equivalent are nearly the same — there are few part-timers. More important, the independent FTE measure shows the same sharp decline in 2023–24, so that turning point is not an artifact of how NYSED counts teachers.
Having confirmed the two agree, the next chart and table repeat the earlier views using the NCES FTE-based ratio — the version directly comparable to other states and to national figures.
On the NCES measure too, Cambridge has the lowest ratio of the group in the two most recent years.


Code

    cc.ccd_ratio_for(cc.GRAPH_GROUP)
    .filter(pl.col("year_end") >= 2018)
    .select("district", "year_end", pl.col("ccd_students_per_teacher").alias("ratio"))
    .drop_nulls()
)
cambridge_ccd = gc.filter(pl.col("district") == cc.FOCUS)

(
    ggplot(gc, aes("year_end", "ratio", color="district"))
    + geom_line(size=1)
    + geom_point(size=1.8)
    + geom_line(cambridge_ccd, size=2.4)  # emphasize the focus district
    + scale_color_manual(
        values=cc.PALETTE, name="", limits=cc.district_order(cc.GRAPH_GROUP)
    )
    + scale_x_continuous(breaks=list(range(2018, 2026)))
    + labs(
        x="School year ending",
        y="Students per teacher (NCES FTE)",
        title="District enrollment ÷ teacher FTE (NCES CCD), 2017–18 to 2024–25",
    )
    + cc.theme_ccs()
)








Figure A.6: NCES (CCD) FTE-based students per teacher by school year ending. Source: NCES Common Core of Data via the Urban Institute Education Data Portal.

title: Appendix B — School district spending
text:
B.1 What “spending per pupil” means here
How much does each district spend to educate a student, and how does Cambridge compare? This chapter opens with the broadest summary — current operating spending per pupil — and will build toward the major categories of spending in later sections.
A district’s spending can be measured several ways, and the choice changes the number a lot. This chapter uses current operating spending: the cost of running schools in a year — salaries, benefits, classroom and BOCES services, food service, and federally funded programs — divided by K-12 enrollment.
It deliberately leaves out two things that make year-to-year comparisons misleading:
This is close to the “current spending per pupil” measure the U.S. Census and the National Center for Education Statistics use to compare districts and states. The dollars come from the New York State Comptroller’s financial-reporting data and are not adjusted for inflation, so part of the upward drift over time reflects rising prices rather than rising real resources.

title: Appendix B — School district spending
text:
Capital projects (new buildings, roofs, buses) and the debt that pays for them. These arrive in large, irregular lumps — one building project can briefly multiply a district’s total spending. Salem’s all-funds spending hit about $87,000 per pupil in 2021–22 for this reason, against current operating spending of about $26,000 that year.
Transfers between a district’s own funds, which would otherwise be counted twice.

title: Appendix B — School district spending
section: B.2 Spending per pupil, over time
text:
B.2 Spending per pupil, over time
Cambridge spends near the top of the group per pupil — consistently second only to Salem.


Code
g = (
    cc.spending_for(cc.GRAPH_GROUP)
    .filter(pl.col("year_end") >= 2016)
    .select("district", "year_end", "current_pp")
    .drop_nulls()
)
cambridge = g.filter(pl.col("district") == cc.FOCUS)

(
    ggplot(g, aes("year_end", "current_pp", color="district"))
    + geom_line(size=1)
    + geom_point(size=1.8)
    + geom_line(cambridge, size=2.4)  # emphasize the focus district
    + scale_color_manual(
        values=cc.PALETTE, name="", limits=cc.district_order(cc.GRAPH_GROUP)
    )
    + scale_x_continuous(breaks=list(range(2016, 2026)))
    + scale_y_continuous(labels=_dollars)
    + labs(
        x="School year ending",
        y="Current operating spending per pupil",
        title="Current operating spending per K-12 pupil, 2015–16 to 2024–25",
    )
    + cc.theme_ccs()
)








Figure B.1: Current operating spending per K–12 pupil (nominal dollars) by school year ending, 2016 (2015–16) through 2025 (2024–25). Source: NYS Comptroller local-government financial data; K-12 enrollment from NYSED.





The two highest-spending districts per pupil — Cambridge and Salem — are also the two smallest by enrollment. That is the cost-side mirror of the students-per-teacher picture: a small district spreads fixed costs (a principal, a building, a bus run) over fewer students, so its spending per pupil runs high even when its total budget is modest. Every district’s figure rises over the decade, but remember these are nominal dollars — much of that climb is inflation.
