title: Sources & references
text:
NYSED data files
The enrollment and staffing numbers are built from two New York State Education Department databases, both downloaded from https://data.nysed.gov/downloads.php and distributed as annual Microsoft Access files:

Enrollment database (New York State Education Department, 2025a) — the BEDS Day Enrollment table, which gives K-12 enrollment by district. Coverage on this website: school years 2015–16 through 2024–25. Enrollment is the BEDS-day count (first Wednesday of October).
Student & Educator (“STUDED”) database (New York State Education Department, 2025b) — the Staff table, which gives teacher, principal, counselor, and social-worker counts plus teacher attendance and turnover. Coverage: school years 2017–18 through 2024–25. Because teacher counts start in 2017–18, any measure that uses teachers (such as students per teacher) begins at school-year-ending

The same database’s Average Class Size table — roster-based class sizes (students ÷ sections) for K-2 and the State-tested grade/subject and Regents courses, school years 2018–19 through 2024–25 — backs the class-size charts; it is measured from course rosters, not teacher counts, and NYSED’s pre-2018-19 (teacher-form) class-size data uses a different method and is excluded.


These were stacked into a district-by-year panel by src/build_enrollment_teachers.py; the dataset’s own SOURCE.md records the build in full.
Caveats worth repeating. num_teachers is a district-wide headcount (all grades, not full-time-equivalents), while k12_enrollment is K-12 only. The derived students-per-teacher ratio is therefore an approximate measure for comparison across districts and over time — not NYSED’s official pupil-teacher ratio or average class size. Charter schools are excluded (they have no district-total row), and a small number of districts reorganized within the window (statewide, not in this comparison group).

title: Sources & references
text:
Student test results (NYSED grades 3-8 assessments)
To look at student performance, the project uses NYSED’s annual Report Card Database (also from https://data.nysed.gov/downloads.php, distributed as Microsoft Access files). These databases hold the results of New York’s grades 3-8 English Language Arts (ELA) and Mathematics tests — the statewide exams every public-school student takes each spring — reported for each district (and for statewide, county, and “similar-needs” peer groupings) and broken out by student group. Coverage built so far: school years 2015–16 through 2024–25 (ten years). The same databases also contain Regents and other results, which may be added later. The ten annual files were stitched into one district-by-year panel by src/build_assessments.py; full provenance is in data/raw/nysed_report_card/SOURCE.md.
The headline measure is the percentage of students scoring at or above proficiency (performance Level 3 or higher); the data also include the number tested and the average score. Two cautions matter a great deal for reading trends:

Spring 2020 is missing and 2021 is unusual. The grades 3-8 tests were cancelled in spring 2020 because of COVID-19, so that year has no data; in spring 2021 only about half of students were tested, so 2021’s numbers are not representative.
The tests were redesigned twice, so scores don’t compare across the breaks. New York set new tests and new passing standards in 2018 and again in 2023 (the Next Generation Learning Standards). Results are comparable within each of three periods — 2015–2017, 2018–2022, and 2023–2025 — but a change in the proficiency rate across one of those boundaries reflects the new standard as much as any change in student learning. Comparisons between districts in the same year are unaffected by this and are the most reliable use of the data.

title: Sources & references
section: National pupil–teacher ratios (NCES Common Core of Data)
text:
National pupil–teacher ratios (NCES Common Core of Data)
To cross-check the headcount-based ratio above against a full-time-equivalent measure, the project pulls the federal Common Core of Data (CCD) school- district directory — which reports teacher FTE — through the Urban Institute’s Education Data Portal (https://educationdata.urban.org), which mirrors NCES. CCD labels a year by its fall term, so it is shifted to this project’s school-year-ending convention (CCD 2024 → 2024–25).

title: Sources & references
text:
School district finance (NYS Comptroller)
Per-pupil spending comes from the New York State Comptroller’s (OSC) local-government financial data — annual revenue and expenditure detail, by account code, that every district reports in its Annual Update Document under the state Uniform System of Accounts. Funds are identified by the leading letter of the account code (General, Special Aid, School Lunch, Capital, Debt Service). The current operating spending per pupil measure used in the spending chapter keeps the General, Special Aid, and School Lunch funds and excludes capital outlay, debt service, and transfers between funds, to approximate the U.S. Census / NCES basis for comparing districts. The fiscal year ends June 30 and matches the school-year-ending label (no off-by-one). Dollars are nominal (not adjusted for inflation). Full provenance: data/raw/osc_school_finance/SOURCE.md.

title: Sources & references
text:
Property Tax Report Card (NYSED)
NYSED posts a statewide Property Tax Report Card workbook each year (https://www.p12.nysed.gov/mgtserv/propertytax/) recording, for every district, the proposed budget, the proposed and prior-year tax levy, the district’s tax levy limit, projected enrollment, and reserve balances — the numbers put before voters each May. These are proposed/budgeted figures (forward- looking), in contrast to the Comptroller’s actual spending. Provenance and the year-by-year download list: data/raw/nysed_property_tax_report_card/SOURCE.md.

title: Sources & references
text:
Budget vote results (NYSED)
NYSED posts statewide School District Budget Voting Results each year (https://www.p12.nysed.gov/mgtserv/votingresults/): the May vote (every district) and the June re-vote (districts that failed the first vote). From these the project derives, per district and vote, the Yes/No counts and a 60%-required flag marking budgets above the tax cap (which must win a supermajority). Together these identify budget defeats, re-votes, and contingency budgets — the subject of the Contingency budgets chapter. These files carry district names (not codes) and no levy figures (only the 60% flag), so they are not joinable to the district crosswalk; cap status comes entirely from the flag. Provenance, the year-by-year URL list, and the heuristic layout-detection parser: data/raw/nysed_budget_votes/SOURCE.md.

title: Sources & references
text:
School district property taxes (NYS Comptroller)
To show how much property tax each school district raises and how heavily it taxes property, the project uses the Comptroller’s “Table 3” school-district tax workbooks (the same OSC local-government data program as the finance files, at https://www.osc.ny.gov/local-government/data/real-property-tax-levies-taxable-full-value-and-full-value-tax-rates). For every district and each year 2015 through 2025, they give the district’s tax levy (total property tax the district raises), its taxable full value (the market-value tax base, after equalizing assessed values across towns), and the full-value tax rate — the levy per $1,000 of full value, the standard way to compare tax effort across places with different assessment practices.
A distinctive feature: each district’s figures are broken out by the city or town the district covers — many districts straddle several towns — so the data has both a detail row per district-and-town and a district total. The totals are summed from the parts (and match the Comptroller’s own published totals, where it publishes them, to the dollar). The fiscal year ends June 30 and matches the school-year-ending label, the same as the finance data (verified against the Property Tax Report Card). Districts are joined to the project’s key via a generated crosswalk; 678 of 680 link, the two exceptions being New York City (which the state splits into many borough districts) and one non-operating district. Full provenance, the format-change history, and caveats (including a 2019-file quirk) are in data/raw/osc_school_district_tax_levies/SOURCE.md.

title: Sources & references
text:
Cambridge CSD annual financial reports
Cambridge’s audited annual financial statements, for fiscal years ended June 30, 2020 through June 30, 2025, were retrieved from the Federal Audit Clearinghouse (https://app.fac.gov/). The clearinghouse was operated by the U.S. Census Bureau through about 2022 and by the U.S. General Services Administration from 2023 onward — which is why the earlier files (FY2020–FY2022) carry a CENSUS tag and bear the title “Financial statements and independent auditor’s report,” while the later files (FY2023–FY2025) carry a GSAFAC tag and the title “Financial report.” They are the same series of district audits.

FY2023 (Cambridge Central School District, 2023), FY2024 (Cambridge Central School District, 2024), FY2025 (Cambridge Central School District, 2025)

title: Sources & references
section: County population and forecast (popfc; Census Bureau)
text:
County population and forecast (popfc; Census Bureau)
The Demographics chapter places Cambridge in its regional population context. New York county population — history and a cohort-component forecast through 2050 — comes from popfc, a sibling Washington County population-forecast project (a cohort-component model whose methodology is documented in its own repository). The baseline scenario is used. Vermont’s Bennington and Rutland counties are not in popfc (it is NYS-only), so their history (2000–2020) is pulled directly from the U.S. Census Bureau’s Population Estimates Program (PEP), July-1 estimates. Unlike the rest of this book, these figures use calendar years (July 1 of each year), not the school-year-ending convention.
Two honest limits shape the chapter: the domestic vs international migration split is available only for history (through 2025) — the forecast projects only total net migration — so the Washington County components-of-change table shows the split through 2025 and net migration thereafter; and the forecast launches from 2024. Full provenance: data/raw/popfc/SOURCE.md and data/raw/census_pep/SOURCE.md.

title: Sources & references
text:
Candidate sources under consideration
These are not yet used on the website but are likely additions, both from New York’s open-data portal (https://data.ny.gov):

Real property tax rates & levy data by municipality (dataset iq85-sdzs) — tax levies and rates by municipality, useful for putting each district’s school tax levy in the context of total local property taxes.

title: Methods & measures
text:
Conventions used throughout
This appendix documents how each measure in the book is defined and its caveats, so the chapters can stay narrative while interested readers can verify the underlying choices. For dataset provenance (publisher, coverage, download lists), see the Sources appendix and the tracked SOURCE.md files under data/raw/.

title: Methods & measures
text:
Year labels are school-year-ending. “2025” means the 2024–25 school year. The one exception is the budget-vote chapter, which indexes by the vote’s calendar year (the May 2024 vote is “2024”); the budget adopted there funds the following school year.
Enrollment is the BEDS-day count — students enrolled on the first Wednesday of October.
Comparison groups. Charts use a 5-district graph group; tables use a 12-district group (every Washington County K-12 district plus Schuylerville and Hoosick Falls). Putnam CSD is excluded because it is K-8 with no high school. See the overview.
Charter schools are excluded — they have no district-total row in the source data, so all figures are district-operated.
Dollars are nominal — not adjusted for inflation — unless noted. Part of any upward drift in money measures reflects rising prices, not rising real resources.
nysed_district_cd (the 8-digit NYSED district code) is the stable join key across datasets; each source’s native id maps to it through a tracked crosswalk. (The budget-vote files are the exception — they carry district names, not codes — so the vote chapter’s Cambridge/neighbor surfacing is by name match.)

title: Methods & measures
text:
Measures and their caveats

Demographics (county population)

County population is the July-1 resident total (Census Bureau basis), shown indexed to a base year (2000 = 100, or 2020 = 100) so the lines compare trends across counties of very different sizes, not raw counts.
New York history and forecast come from the sibling popfc cohort- component project (baseline scenario); Vermont counties (Bennington, Rutland) come straight from the Census Bureau, which has no popfc forecast. These are the book’s only calendar-year figures — the one place that departs from the school-year-ending convention.
Components of change (births, deaths, migration) are shown for Washington County only. The domestic vs international migration split is available only for history (through 2025); the forecast projects just total net migration, so the split columns are blank for forecast years. 2020 is omitted from the components table because the 2020 Census boundary makes that year a partial April–July period in the source. Forecast lines (from 2025) are dashed.



Enrollment and teachers

K-12 enrollment — BEDS-day count (above).
Teacher counts — a NYSED headcount (the number of people employed as teachers, all grades K-12), not full-time-equivalents. Teacher counts are available from 2017–18, so any series that depends on them (such as students per teacher) begins at school-year-ending 2018, even though enrollment runs back to 2015–16.
Students per teacher = K-12 enrollment ÷ teacher headcount. It is an approximation for cross-district and over-time comparison, not NYSED’s official class-size figure and not a count of children in a typical classroom.



Class size

NYSED “average class size” is roster/section-based — students in a section ÷ number of sections — reported only for a handful of specific grades and subjects, and only since 2018–19. Where the book summarizes it, it takes the median across the reported classes to resist small-section artifacts (a 2-student physics class, a pandemic-year glitch).
It is distinct from the headcount-based students per teacher (above) and the FTE-based NCES ratio (below).



Student test results (grades 3–8 ELA & Math)

Proficiency rate = (students at levels 3+4+5) ÷ (students tested). This reproduces NYSED’s own reported proficiency rate exactly in the modern-layout years.
Comparability has limits. New York reset its tests and passing standards twice, so rates compare only within three eras — 2015–17 (Common Core), 2018–22 (redesign), 2023–25 (Next Generation) — not across them. A change across an era boundary reflects the new standard as much as any change in learning.
2020 is absent (tests cancelled) and 2021 is anomalous (only about half of students participated; not representative).



Spending per pupil

Current operating spending per pupil = (General + Special Aid + School Lunch funds) minus capital outlay, debt service, and interfund transfers, divided by K-12 enrollment. It captures salaries, benefits, classroom and BOCES services, food service, and federally funded programs.
What it excludes, and why: capital projects and the debt that funds them arrive in large, irregular lumps (they would make spending per pupil volatile), and interfund transfers would otherwise be counted twice.
This is close to the “current spending per pupil” measure the U.S. Census and NCES use. Dollars are nominal.



Property taxes

Tax levy — the total property tax the district collects.
Full-value tax rate = levy ÷ taxable full value × 1,000 — the standard way to compare tax effort across places with different assessment practices.
The rate can move sharply with a reassessment (which expands the tax base and lowers the rate even as the levy rises) — e.g., Washington County’s reassessment around 2022–23.



Property-tax cap

A district may raise its levy by the lesser of 2% or CPI without special action; to exceed it the budget must win a 60% supermajority.
Each district’s annual levy limit is set by the state formula; certain growth (pension contributions, capital projects, court-ordered payments) can be excluded from the cap.
The over/under gap is computed here as proposed levy − levy limit (both excluding permissible exclusions). The source workbook’s stored levy_vs_limit field is not used because NYSED flips its sign at year_end 2016.



Reserves and fund balance

Reserve funds are restricted accounts (A814–A884), each created under a specific statute for a specific purpose. Fund balance is classified restricted / committed / assigned (appropriated or not) / unassigned.
The 4% cap (tax-cap law) applies to the unassigned fund balance only — not to the restricted reserve funds. So a district can hold large restricted reserves and still be “at the cap.”
Per-reserve legal restrictions govern use: the capital reserve requires voter approval to establish and to expend; the tax-certiorari reserve is mandatory, capped at anticipated claims, with unused funds returned to the General Fund; the retirement reserve may be funded up to 2% of covered salaries per year (TRS sub-fund capped at 10%).



Budget votes and contingency budgets

A budget passes at ≥60% yes if it is above the cap, else at a simple majority (>50%). A district is on contingency if it failed the first vote and then either failed the re-vote or held none.
Cap status comes from NYSED’s “60% required” flag — the vote files carry no levy figures, only that flag.
Pre-2012 is omitted (the tax cap took effect in 2012, so the above/below-cap split is undefined before then), and 2020 is atypical (all-absentee, shifted re-vote date).

title: Methods & measures
text:
Cross-checks and validation

NYSED headcount vs NCES FTE (students per teacher). The book’s NYSED headcount-based ratio and the independent NCES CCD full-time-equivalent ratio never differ by more than about two-thirds of a student per teacher, and both show the same sharp 2023–24 decline — so that turning point is not an artifact of how NYSED counts teachers.
Contingency defeats vs NYSED’s statewide totals. Computed first-vote defeat counts match NYSED’s own published P/D statewide totals exactly for 2021–2025 (within one for the anomalous 2020).
OSC ST-3 vs audited reports (reserves). The as-filed OSC account-code reserve balances reconcile with Cambridge’s audited annual financial reports (the ~$8,000 difference is a school-lunch encumbrance).
PTRC % Yes reconciliation. Where NYSED’s vote/levy workbooks include a “% Yes” column, the parsed Yes/No counts rebuild it to 0.0000 median error.

title: Methods & measures
text:
Limitations — where comparisons break down

No inflation adjustment in money measures — part of the upward drift is prices, not real growth.
Small districts are volatile. Cambridge, Salem, and the other small peers have small counts in some cuts of the data (high-school sections, single test grades), so individual values can swing; the book prefers medians and notes this where it matters.
Per-pupil spending is mechanically higher for small districts because fixed costs (a principal, a building, a bus route) spread over fewer students — a structural feature of scale, not inefficiency, and central to Cambridge’s story.
Name-based matching for budget votes (no codes) means a handful of re-votes each year don’t link to a first-vote record in the older files; re-vote-outcome counts are therefore reliable from ~2020 on and approximate before.
Test-score eras and the tax cap each create discrete breaks in their series (above) — trends must be read within an era, not across.
